text: There's a lot more to do in Rome than just drool  over ruins and stairs, so in today's video, I'll be sharing a thorough roundup of things to do in  this epic city, with honest opinions on what's worth it. In case you're new here, hi, I'm Christina  from HappyToWander.com and this channel is all about practical travel tips from someone who's  been there and done that, so be sure to like and subscribe if that sounds like your kind of  thing. And stick around until the end for a free map that plots all these activities along  with recommendations for cafes and restaurants. Now let's start with an important must-doo before  seeing the sights and that's to gorge yourself on Roman dishes. Rome is responsible for some of  the tastiest and most popular pasta dishes on the planet, so it makes sense that when in Rome  you need to slurp your way through them all with reckless abandon. Pasta carbonara is a popular one  that is already globally famous, made with eggs cheese and cured pork but other must-tries include  cacio pepe, which is made with cheese and pepper, pasta amatriciana, which is made with tomato, cheese  and cured pork, and pasta alla gricia, which is made with

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN ROME FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20 Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: cheese, black pepper and cured pork. I know this all  sounds very simple but the beauty of Roman cuisine is in its simplicity. If you're looking for non-  pasta dishes to try, artichokes, tripe and oxtail are also very popular. As is the almighty trapizzino, a beloved Roman street food that combines pizza and sandwiches for a thrilling triangular result  that is perfect as a quick bite on the go. And of course, there's plenty of great coffee and gelato  to enjoy as well, along with plenty of rooftop bars where you can soak in the sights from a unique  vantage point. Now on that note, let's move on to the main attractions and sites starting  with the top must-do which is to tour the Colosseum. Possibly the most famous monument in  Rome, the Roman Colosseum is the largest standing amphitheater in the world, dating back almost  2,000 years. For centuries, it was here that Rome's infamous gladiator fights and animal hunts took  place, often to crowds as large as 80,000. Today it can receive up to 30,000 visitors a day making  it one of the most visited monuments in the entire world, which is why you should 100% make sure  you get tickets in advance or even book an after

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN ROME FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20 Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: hours tour for a unique less crowded experience.  I can definitely recommend paying extra to tour the underground arena bit, which only opened to  the public a few years ago. It really does help you understand how the arena would have worked and  operated back in the day. Now next on our list of Rome must-dos is to see the Roman Forum and Palatine  Hill. A visit here is included in the ticket you buy for the Colosseum, so you might as well. Long  the epicenter of commercial and government activity in ancient Rome, this multi-purpose space  would have been used for countless aspects of daily life, from elections and social gatherings  to religious ceremonies and criminal trials. To be honest, this is a space that can be tough to  appreciate without the help of a guide since these days you're mainly looking at ruins with minimal  signage, so consider reading up on the history of the space in advance or potentially hiring a  tour guide to help you get some much needed context. Close to here, you'll find another wonder  of ancient Rome: Trajan's Market, an impressive second century complex spanning six stories and over  150 rooms, considered to be the world's first

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN ROME FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20 Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: ever shopping mall. Besides cool ruins the site  is also home to the Museum of the Imperial Forums where you can learn more about the forums that  once occupied this space. Another highlight here is the almost 2,000-year-old Trajan's Column, whose  impressive details actually tell the epic story of Emperor Trajan's victory in the 2nd century Dacian  Wars, a victory so huge they apparently partied for 123 days after. Now next on our ancient Rome hit  list is to visit the Pantheon. Of all the buildings that remain from ancient Rome the Pantheon is by  far the best preserved, dazzling visitors even 2,000 years after it was originally constructed.  And there's a lot of reasons to visit, after all, the Pantheon houses tombs for some of Rome's  most famous figures including Italy's first king: King Vittorio Emanuele II and Ninja Turtle  namesake the famous painter Raphael. It's also home to the largest unsupported concrete dome in  the world, weighing in at a shocking 4,535 tons. This is definitely one of the most incredible places  to visit in Rome, so make sure you add it to your list. And if you're not tired of ancient Rome  quite yet then be sure to check out the city's

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN ROME FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20 Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: less famous ruins. While not as epic and well-known  as some of the sites mentioned above, some other ancient Roman sites to visit include the Circus  Maximus where chariot races once took place. As well as the Terme di Caracalla, once among the largest  public baths in Rome and today open to the public for visits, which includes a virtual guide  that helps you visualize what the complex would have looked like back in the day. There's also  the cat sanctuary built on the site of where Caesar was murdered, over in the Largo di Torre Argentina,  set among an ensemble of modern shops, cafes and restaurants as well as maybe my personal favorite:  a McDonald's with its own piece of Roman Wall located inside Rome's Termini Station, of all places!  This is possibly the only place in the world you can wolf down big nuggets while sitting next to  a 2500-year old chunk of Roman wall, so definitely worth checking out if even just for a quick  peek. Now leaving ancient Rome behind, it's time to tackle another one of Rome's top must-dos, commonly  considered to be a disappointing one and that's to admire Piazza di Spagna and its Spanish Steps. As one  of the most famous sites in Rome, many visitors

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN ROME FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20 Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: are surprised to see they are indeed just 135  photogenic steps and not much else but in their defense, they are iconic, so worth a quick look. Just  beware that they're always very packed with people, so coming early in the morning or late at night  is the only way to really appreciate them properly. By the way, know that you also can't sit or eat on  them as many whistle-armed guards will remind you! Now speaking of overcrowded places you should  visit early in the morning, another Rome must-do is the Trevi Fountain, perhaps one of the most  famous fountains in the entire world. At 65 feet wide this monumental fountain is best known  for its impressive marble sculptures and, of course, its dreamy blue hues. These days millions  of tourists come for a chance every year to ogle the fountain and partake in the classic tourist  ritual of throwing a coin, or three, over their left shoulder: the first to ensure a return to Rome,  the second for romance and the third for marriage. It is a beautiful fountain but always swarming  with people, so again plan accordingly. From the Trevi Fountain it's only a two-minute walk over to  one of the prettiest hidden courtyards in Rome:

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN ROME FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20 Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: Galleria Sciarra. This unassuming walkway tucked behind  a McDonald's, no less, is a lovely hidden gem full of beautiful frescoes and artwork to admire, with a  gorgeous glass and iron ceiling that makes the space feel impossibly grand, especially when you  consider that it's simply home to offices these days. Now next on our list is to pay a visit to  Piazza del Popolo. Translated as the People's Square, Piazza del Popolo is considered one of Rome's most  important and aesthetically pleasing squares, housing a number of attractions including the  Leonardo da Vinci Museum and several churches. Another must-see square in Rome is the baroque  masterpiece known as Piazza Navona, with a unique oblong shape thanks to its history as the  former site of the Domitian Stadium, which was built back in the 1st century to introduce  Greek-style athletics to the Roman public, who's main source of entertainment at the  time was pretty much just watching slaves murder each other with cameos from lions,  tigers and bears. Unfortunately, Romans far preferred their gladiator games, so the  stadium fell into disuse over the years eventually being transformed in the  17th century into a gorgeous baroque piazza. Another must-see for history lovers in the  city is Castel Sant'Angelo. Few monuments in Rome

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN ROME FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20 Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: have evolved with the city's history and power  struggles as much as this one, which has been around since the 2nd century. Originally built as a  mausoleum for Emperor Hadrian, it was later converted into a castle by the popes, who from the 14th  century onwards began to add their own little flourishes like chapels and statues to glam up  the place to their liking. Luckily, they got a lot of use out of it, not only has Castel Sant'Angelo  been a fortress and safe haven over the years, it has also been used as a prison and more recently,  even a film set where it starred as a key location in Angels and Demons as well as Eat Pray Love.  Now, next on our list is to see the Vittoriano, also known as the Altar of the Fatherland or more  whimsically: the wedding cake or typewriter. This pristine marble monument was constructed in  the late 19th century to commemorate Vittorio Emanuele II, the first king of a unified Italy. It  was officially inaugurated in 1911, 40 years after unification, with a design inspired by Hellenistic  monuments like the Pergamon Altar. Since 1921, the monument has also housed the Tomb of the Unknown  Soldier; a shrine dedicated to those who perished

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN ROME FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20 Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: and sacrificed their lives during World War I.  There's also some nice museums inside. This is definitely a monument worth checking out in Rome  because it's completely free to visit, with only a small additional fee for the elevator ride up the  terrace if you so choose, so be sure to check it out. Now, let's move on to one of the most popular  Rome activities and that's to visit Vatican City. Nestled within the heart of Rome, Vatican City  stands as the world's smallest independent State, as well as headquarter of the Roman Catholic  Church. This microstate is home to the pope and several points of interest, including the largest  church in the world: St Peter's Basilica, with a dome that reaches just under 450 feet. This is truly  one of the most stunning churches in the world, filled with ornate details and magnificent works  of art, as well as an excellent view at the top if you can brave the climb. If you can time your  visit to coincide with the hourly mark, you'll also get to hear the church bells ring from  Rome's hundreds of churches at the same time, which is a pretty special experience. Granted,  it does get very narrow at the top and there's a lot of steps, so don't attempt it unless you're  reasonably fit. From this view you get a great look

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN ROME FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20 Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: over St Peter's Square, where you'll be greeted by  the monumental Obelisk and surrounded by elegant colonnades designed by Bernini. This square is  where the papal audience is held at certain times of the week and today, mainly just a spot  for photos and lining up to get into St Peter's. Be aware that lines can get very long here, so  I plan to visit first thing in the morning and don't forget about the strict dress code as  well, which requires you to have both knees and shoulders covered. The other main attraction here  in Vatican City are the Vatican Museums, which house an unparalleled collection of art and historical  treasures, including the world-famous Sistine Chapel. I'll be honest though, this museum while stunning,  is almost always packed to the brim with people, so much that you aren't really able to appreciate  the pieces at all and are instead herded around like cattle, especially in the Sistine Chapel where  they constantly shush you with no mercy. With peak season crowds it's really not the best use of  time, in my opinion, unless you are really, really interested in art. There's also the Vatican Gardens,  a tranquil oasis within this bustling city-state, as well as an admittedly cheesier stop: the post  office where you can send a postcard through

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN ROME FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20 Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: Vatican City's own postal service with its own  unique stamps. Just make sure you don't forget a pen. Now having discussed St Peter's, the largest  church in the world, now feels like a good time to mention another Rome activity which is to  explore some of Rome's 900-plus churches. Yes, there really are that many, which explains   why we don't really have time to name them all. My honest recommendation is to just wander around  and have a look inside churches that catch your interest, so that way you can find your own hidden  gems but here are a few unique churches to add to your list that I think are really cool. Firstly,  there's the Chiesa di Sant'Ignazio di Loyola, which is home to incredible frescoes that offer a cool and  unique optical illusion, as well as the Basilica de Sant'Andrea della Valle, known for its massive dome  and also amazing frescoes. For those looking for a darker experience there's also the Santa Maria della  Concezione dei Cappuccini, known for its Capuchin Crypt, which is filled with chapels artfully decorated  with bones; and the Basilica of Santa Maria in Cosmedin, another popular one thanks to some unique  features like the School of St Valentine and also

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN ROME FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20 Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: the famous Bocca della Veritá, an ancient marble mask said  to bite the hands of liars. To be honest, this mask isn't anything super remarkable, especially  given there's almost always a long lineup to take a photo with it, so I wouldn't necessarily  go out of my way to see it. The church though, is pretty cool. Now one place I would go out of my  way to see though is the amazing Villa Borghese, one of Rome's prettiest and largest public parks  and also home to a number of villas and museums, including the Galleria Borghese, one of Rome's most  famous art galleries with an impressive collection spread across 20 rooms and two floors. Another  highlight of the park is a quick but affordable rowboat paddle, though be warned that the lake  is 100% filled with other tourists who have no idea how to row boats, so the experience is less  than Notebook and more like an on-water edition of bumper cars. Lastly, while here don't forget to  soak in the beautiful views from Terraza del Pincio, this is one of the best panoramic views in the  city and a great place to get your bearings when in town. It's also a highly popular sunset spot, so  you might want to consider coming back later with a bottle of wine in hand. Speaking of great views,  you should also make sure to visit the Giardino

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN ROME FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20 Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: degli Aranci, also known as Parco Savello. This leafy garden  on Aventine Hill is lined with its namesake orange trees, with a gorgeous panoramic view over the  city, especially at sunset. This is a great place to relax and take in the views plus very close to  here is the Buco della serratura dell’Ordine di Malta AKA Rome's Keyhole, a popular photo opp with a unique view of St  Peter's Basilica that you can access through a keyhole which is perfectly framed by hedges to  create a surreal and dreamy postcard view,. Though be warned, it's actually pretty tough to photograph.  Of course, with Rome famously built on seven hills there are many other great spots to enjoy a  view in the city, including Janiculum, just south of the Vatican, which has its own beautiful monumental  fountain. Now up until now, we've discussed a lot of big sites to see but it's important to  note that another fun Rome must-do is to visit one of the city's many museums. Some of the most  popular ones include the Musei Capitolini, which are considered the oldest public museums in the  world. There's also the stunning Borghese Gallery, as previously mentioned, and the Palazzo Doria Pamphilj, which is home to an elegant art museum. As well

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN ROME FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20 Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: as many, many others, so if you're looking for more  things to do in Rome, especially on a rainy day, be sure to add museum hopping to your list. Finally  it has to be said that Rome is an amazing city and while most visitors will spend their time  exploring the most central and touristic parts, there are tons of gorgeous neighborhoods beloved  by the millions of Romans who call it home, so if time allows be sure to check out some more  neighborhoods outside of the central core. Some nice neighborhoods to explore include the Trastavere  neighborhood, home to tons of beautiful streets and restaurants; the unique Quartiere Coppedè, which is tucked  away in the Trieste district with a mish-mash of varying architectural styles, and Testaccio, home to  Rome's last remaining pyramid. Now finally, if you're up for a cool day trip Rome does have  quite a few options as well. While most companies will try to sell you on a really long day trip  to Pompeii or to the Amalfi Coast, my personal pick would be something closer like Tivoli, which is  home to a few elegant villas and magnificent views that you can reach in as quickly as 40  minutes. There's also many cool sites closer to the city including catacombs, the Appian Way - also known as Europe's longest straight road -

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN ROME FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20 Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: and more relics of ancient Rome like  aqueducts. Now, while this video has only just scraped the surface of what to do in  Rome, I do hope you enjoyed it. Be sure to check out this link for a free map of all the  places I mentioned and like and subscribe for more practical travel videos just like  this one. I'll see you guys next week, bye!

text: For the French, the Louvre is more than just a museum. What was once a royal palace became a public treasure house that France prides itself for. It represents the country's history and sits at the center of French culture and identity. That's why it was supposed to be untouchable. The cultural and historical value of the Louvre turned it into an impregnable fortress guarded by scanners, cameras, and a small army of staff. It was believed to be the most secure place for any priceless piece of art. Yet, on the quiet Sunday morning of October 19th, that illusion came crashing down. In a heist that lasted only 7 minutes, a crew slipped in through the second floor window of the Galerie d'Apollon, like a scene from Ocean's 11, and emptied two cases of crown jewels. By noon, streets were shut off. The French public was in shambles, and every screen in Paris carried the same headline. A sprawling international manhunt underway this morning after thieves took priceless jewels from the Louvre Museum in Paris. This wasn't just a robbery, it was a strike at France's history and its national pride. This is how the Louvre got hit in broad daylight. [Music]

meta.video_title: The Louvre | The Wildest Robbery of the 21st Century

text: For a long time, the Louvre has felt bulletproof. A palace turned museum that seemed beyond risk. However, this is not the first heist the Louvre has known. In 1911, Vincenzo Peruggia, an ordinary handyman, lifted the Mona Lisa off the wall, hid it, and turned it into a global obsession. After a 2-year-long search, the painting was found in Italy and restored to its place at the museum. Since then, security was rebuilt, and the Louvre has proven to have earned the status of an impregnable citadel. The masterpieces of art and national symbols it held were safely kept behind layers of protection. Cameras tucked along the walls, locked doors, and guard routes planned to the minute. The museum ran like a clock with controlled entrances and a 24/7 operation center. Despite all of this, the unthinkable happened when everyone least expected it. The heist took place in the Galerie d'Apollon, a hall that houses what is left of the French crown jewels, necklaces, and ceremonial pieces tied to French nobility. The gallery was not remote. It is located in the inner circle of the museum, only 250 m away from the Mona Lisa. In this zone, everything says safe. Crowds of tourists, thick glass panes, vibration

meta.video_title: The Louvre | The Wildest Robbery of the 21st Century

text: sensors on the cases, motion alarms in the corners, and guards who study every move. The one place you'd swear no one would dare to rob, and that's exactly why the hit felt more like a heist movie than a real incident. But, here's the twist. This was not a sophisticated high-tech job with lasers and explosives. It was brilliantly simple. A lift, a window, a saw, and 7 ruthless minutes. It was clear that the heist crew did their homework beforehand. They chose Sunday morning, the quietest hour of the week, when foot traffic is low and response times are longer. They came dressed like French workmen in their famous yellow jackets and rolled up an ordinary basket lift to the Seine-facing facade. The placement was chosen with meticulous care. Near a construction zone, exactly where a lift wouldn't look out of place, and just far enough from the main flow of guards. At roughly 9:30 a.m., three or four men went up the lift to an upper floor window and forced it open with a blowtorch and a small chainsaw. Once inside the Galerie d'Apollon, they didn't wander or hesitate. They beelined to two specific cases with their grinders in hand. The grinders roared to life, and in under 4

meta.video_title: The Louvre | The Wildest Robbery of the 21st Century

text: to 7 minutes, the reinforced glass turned to dust under them. This is a real video shot by a tourist of one of the thieves while trying to get through to one of the jewels. The thieves managed to scoop nine specific jewels, a tiara, a brooch, and a crown belonging to Empress Eugénie, wife of Napoleon III, and a bunch of other jewels similar in value. Alarms did sound, and protocols were followed. We were just ready to go in to see the Mona Lisa when they swept us out of the gallery. Swept us down the stairs by the Winged Victory. Then we were headed out the exit. We didn't realize the whole museum was being evacuated. Security locked down the room, avoiding a physical fight with the robbers. They then started the evacuation of the museum immediately while waiting for the police. However, the delay between the breach and full response was all the thieves needed to get in, grab the jewels, and then retrace their steps out of the window. This is a real video filmed by a tourist of the thieves descending the lift. They escaped on the back of two waiting scooters. But, not everything went according to plan. In their rush, a 19th-century

meta.video_title: The Louvre | The Wildest Robbery of the 21st Century

text: crown was dropped or discarded during their escape and later found by authorities in a nearby alley lying damaged on the ground. Inside the Louvre, police sealed entrances and the museum closed for the day. Forensics teams lifted prints from the lift's controls, pulled surveillance footage from interior corridors and exterior streets. To make sure not to be linked to the robbery, the thieves had rented out the lift truck 9 days prior. They placed the order under a fake name, and when the delivery guy came to deliver the lift truck, two masked guys showed up to the location and hijacked the truck. They painted it and put up a fake license plate. The initial investigation conclusion was quickly shaped. Experienced professionals exploited a construction site adjacent to the museum as an access to perform a high-level smash and grab. The thieves were last seen heading south on their scooters, likely aiming for the highway out of Paris altogether. The first part of their mission was successful, but what comes next is way more challenging. With all of France on their tail, will they be able to melt the pieces and see their money, or will all of it be for nothing? France woke up to more than a theft. It was a blow to national pride that caused a political outcry, with Macron calling the raid an attack on our

meta.video_title: The Louvre | The Wildest Robbery of the 21st Century

text: history. National Rally leader Jordan Bardella said it was an intolerable humiliation, and Marine Le Pen of Front National called it a wound to the French soul. What's even worse, this is not the first French museum to be hit. Since September, three museums have been hit in Paris. The National Museum of Natural History, the Museum of President Jacques Chirac, and now the Louvre. The pressure on authorities is at an all-time high to put an end to this humiliating pattern and appease the French public. Solving this heist is a race against the clock. Pieces this famous can't be sold intact, which pushes thieves towards breaking them up, melting them, and recutting their stones. That's why the first week following any museum heist is the most important. If pieces are recovered during it, they are usually still intact and can be saved. But, beyond the 1-week mark, pieces are typically already gone for good. 800 million euros are on the line. So, they planned this very well with inside help, for sure. What they could do, there's three ways to do it. They could break them all up, sell the gold, sell the diamond and listen, there's a lot of diamonds. That's one way. Then, they could also wait a while, and a lot of times the insurance companies will put out a reward. A no questions asked if they, you know, it's

meta.video_title: The Louvre | The Wildest Robbery of the 21st Century

text: a $5 million hit, they'll put out a million dollars right now, and you know, people do that. They actually wait. >> Up to now, the thieves have left behind their scooters, the lift, and a yellow vest worn during the heist. With only a few clues available, things are not looking good for the French. Can France catch the thieves and save the jewels before history gets melted down? A question only time can answer. text: The Louvre is home to some of the world's most famous masterpieces, including the "Mona Lisa" and "Venus de Milo." This 60,000 square meter exhibition space is the world’s largest museum, and holds over 35,000 pieces of art. It is said that if you spend 30 seconds looking at each piece, it would take about 4 months. And that’s non-stop, 24 hours a day. But visiting the Louvre is one of the most enjoyable things to do when you’re in Paris. From the Roman and Greek antiquities, to Egyptian, African, Oceanic and Islamic arts, there’s something for everyone. To help your visit as you go through this majestic place brimming with culture and history, here are a few suggestions. The first thing you’ll see on your visit, is  this large glass and metal structure. The Louvre pyramid has become one of the most recognizable Parisian landmark. It was designed by the Chinese-American architect I.M. Pei in 1988 as a part of an expansion project. The French president at the time, François Mitterrand,  initiated this Project in 1981, to add on the Richelieu Wing of the palace, that was previously home of the Ministry of Finance.

meta.video_title: 10 Artworks to See at the Louvre Museum | Behind the Masterpiece

text: This decade long project doubled the exhibition space, with the Pyramid as the new entrance. But it was not as loved then as it is today. Some argued that its modernist style clashed with the classic French renaissance style of the art and history of the museum. Others were hesitant about trusting a Chinese-American architect who was unfamiliar with French culture, to take on the massive renovation of a treasured French landmark. Today it stands as one of France’s most important architectural icons. Welcoming millions of visitors every year, it is almost synonymous with the Louvre. The first piece of artwork everyone is heading towards, the most famous artwork in history really, is the Mona Lisa. Millions crowd around this rather small painting every year to snap a photo. Knowing the power it holds on society today, it’s shocking to know that up until the beginning of the 20th century, the "Mona Lisa" was actually relatively unknown outside of the art world. Its sudden rise to fame occurred in 1911 when a Louvre employee, Vincenzo Peruggia, stole the portrait to bring it back to his home country of Italy where he believed it belonged. He kept the painting hidden in his apartment for two year before presenting it to the Uffizi

meta.video_title: 10 Artworks to See at the Louvre Museum | Behind the Masterpiece

text: Gallery in Florence, where it was hung for only two weeks before it was returned to the Louvre. It wasn’t gone for very long, but that was enough time for this previously looked-over painting to be thrusted into the spotlight. This oil painting by Leonardo da Vinci, is a portrait of a woman seated in front of an imaginary landscape. The background is done in the Sfumato painting technique, which is the softening of transitions between colours and tones. We see that here through hazy outlines, blurred forms, and the absence of clear lines. The figure who is believed to be Lisa Gherardini, the wife of a silk merchant, sits at the centre with her arms folded, as she gazes at the viewer with a soft smile. A smile whose ambiguous nature has made this iconic painting even more enigmatic, enticing the viewers to try and understand both the intention of da Vinci and his muse. Eugene Delacroix, a leader of Romanticism, painted  "Liberty Leading People" in 1830, the same year of the Second French Revolution, also known as The Three Glorious Days. This conflict was due to the differing views on who should rule France. For three days, working class citizens fought the French royal army,

meta.video_title: 10 Artworks to See at the Louvre Museum | Behind the Masterpiece

text: abdicating King Charles the tenth, and creating a constitutional monarchy, radically altering the course of French history. This large-scale painting provides visual insight into this major historical event. It is an allegorical painting on the concept of liberty, embodied by the female figure atop the crowd, who is believed to be Marianne, a personification of the French Republic. The other figures are placed in the shape of a pyramid, with Marianne at the top, emphasizing her importance to the viewers whose gaze start and end with her. She is holding the national flag and is wearing a Phrygian cap, which was a garment that was historically chosen by freed slaves in Greece and Rome for its symbolism, that of liberty. Behind her, a diverse crowd from all walks of life are amidst celebrating their victory. The artist depicted this imaginary scene to show his support for the ones who have risked their lives during the Second Revolution for the future of France. “I have undertaken a modern subject, a barricade, and although I may not have fought for my country, at least I shall have painted for her.”

meta.video_title: 10 Artworks to See at the Louvre Museum | Behind the Masterpiece

text: Painted by Paolo Veronese in 1563, this large-scale  painting sits opposite to the "Mona Lisa," and often goes unnoticed because of its  location. It is painted in a Mannerist style, an art movement that  exaggerates classic characteristics of the Renaissance art movement. Mannerism is depicted through asymmetrical and  unnatural choices of light, colors, and figures. Filled with dozens of subjects, this piece shows the biblical story of the Marriage at Cana, which was attended by Mary, Jesus of Nazareth and some of his apostles. During the wedding ceremony, the supply of wine ends, and at Mary’s request, Jesus commands the servants to fill stone jugs with water, which he then transformed into wine. That is what we are seeing here. The moment of this miracle, in a grand style of a feast of food, music and people. This painting is the largest artwork at the Louvre, and was brought to the museum in 1797, over 200 years after it was completed. Originally, it was hung at the San Giorgio Monastery in Venice, but under Napoleon’s reign during the French revolutionary wars, his army took this piece as spoils of war.

meta.video_title: 10 Artworks to See at the Louvre Museum | Behind the Masterpiece

text: And that proved to be a difficult task. Due to its substantial size, the soldiers had to cut this artwork down the middle, and roll it so it could  be brought back to France, where it was to be reassembled. This exquisite marble masterpiece graces the top of the Daru stairway, for all to see as they move through the building. Its placement adds onto its allure. Located in this modern space, she almost looks like she is floating. Like many other antiquities, its origins were essentially unknown when it was unearthed, but experts have conducted research for years, and now believe that it was likely crafted by the people of Rhodes, a Greek island, in the early second century BC. This 18-foot sculpture depicts Nike, the Greek goddess of victory, triumphantly stepping forward to the front of a ship during a successful sea battle. We can see how much the artist has cared for this piece. The hard texture of the marble somehow looks wet and wind-blown, as Nike’s seemingly translucent garment clings to her, twisting around her waist and wrapping around her legs. Discovered in 1863 by French diplomat and archaeologist, this statue

meta.video_title: 10 Artworks to See at the Louvre Museum | Behind the Masterpiece

text: was originally only three pieces. The base, torso, and legs. The museum made a choice to add on the wings, which were only partially found, and stopped there, choosing not to recreate the head or the arms. Almost a century later, archaeologists from Austria found the figure’s right hand. As the figure is armless, there was no way to reattach it to the sculpture, and they didn’t. But unearthing this open palm was still significant, as it disproved theories that the figure originally had been holding an object. Throughout the years, this masterpiece has inspired other artists. Like the Surrealist Salvador Dalí directly and Futurist Umberto Boccioni but no other piece can captivate audiences as powerfully as the original. "The Code of Hammurabi" is an artifact of Ancient Mesopotamia  and earliest collection of written laws, 282 to be exact, in the history of mankind. The text covers various rules and punishments for theft, agriculture, women's rights, slave rights, murder and death. In fact, The common phrase of “An eye for an eye ...” is

meta.video_title: 10 Artworks to See at the Louvre Museum | Behind the Masterpiece

text: actually paraphrased from this text. Created by King Hammurabi in Babylon in 1760 BC, it was brought to Susa, 3,000 years later as spoils of war. The stele is made of Basalt— an extremely hard volcanic rock that's very difficult to work with —making the accuracy of the intricate engravings even more impressive. The figure on the top left is King Hammurabi himself, symbolically receiving the laws from the sun god on the right, Shamash, who was the patron of Justice, depicted by the flames behind him. The two figures are portrayed in a rigid stance. This was typical of Babylonian art which aimed to portray recognizable characters and scenes, without the desire to make them look real and life-like. Previously, rules were passed down generations orally. Hammurabi was the first ruler who decided to convert rules into written laws on record to be available to all. This courtyard holds the remains of the city of “Dûr-Sharrukin” or “The fortress of Sargon.” It was ordered to be constructed by King Sargon II in late 8th century BC, and was to be the

meta.video_title: 10 Artworks to See at the Louvre Museum | Behind the Masterpiece

text: new capital of the Assyrian empire. It was built at the foot of the mount Musri in present day Iraq, and measured at 1758.6 by 1635 metres, making it the largest city in the ancient world. It was worked on for 10 years, but was unfortunately never completed. Its construction came to an abrupt stop when King Sargon II died in a battle. The site was then rediscovered in 1843 by the French vice-consul in Mosul. "The Lamassu" were among the pieces that were excavated and brought back to France. These magical creatures take the form of hybrid beasts with bodies and ears of winged bulls, the wings of an eagle, and heads of human males which resemble depictions of Sargon II. They are Mesopotamian protective entities, and have the combined powers of the many different animals their bodies take the form of. Yet they are benevolent as seen from their gentle smiles. The roles of these gigantic alabaster figures was to watch over the city, should the city  have been completed and preserved, the Lamassu would be carved at important doorways, in order to provide protection. This marble

meta.video_title: 10 Artworks to See at the Louvre Museum | Behind the Masterpiece

text: masterpiece is famous for its beauty, and recognized for its missing arms. But like many other artifacts, not much was known about this piece when it was unearthed in the 19th century on the Aegean island of Melos. Thanks to the years of research conducted, experts now have pieced together a narrative. It is said to have been created by Alexandros of Antioch, a Greek sculptor, sometime during 150-125 BC. This sculpture is  made of Parian marble, which is a fine-grained semi translucent pure-white material, that’s found in quarries on the island of Paros, Greece. It features a semi-nude female figure, posed in a classical S-curve, bending one way first and then back the other. The identity of this larger-than-life statue has remained a mystery. Since it was discovered without its arms, it’s nearly impossible to confirm with who she’s supposed to represent as many artistic depictions of Greek goddesses contain clues to their identity in the  characteristics of their hands. Some believe it to be Aphrodite, the goddess of beauty. This is due to her uncovered body, and the statue’s proximity to a hand holding an apple when it was discovered, which is an attribute of hers. Others believe it to be the Amphitrite,

meta.video_title: 10 Artworks to See at the Louvre Museum | Behind the Masterpiece

text: the sea goddess that was worshipped on the island of Melos where it was found. Either way, this timeless masterpiece is to remain at the Louvre to educate and enamour for centuries to come. This painting is extremely significant in its aesthetics, choice of subject matter, the artist, and the historical and societal context in which it was created. Portrait of Madeleine, formerly named Portrait of a Black Woman as the subject was previously unidentified, was painted in 1800, during a brief  period in which France had abolished colonial slavery. At that time, paintings of heroic black figures were gaining popularity. This created the perfect opening for a female artist, of which there was few, to paint an image of a black woman who is the centre of her own portrait. She is her own individual, to be looked at, and not looked over. Previously, black subjects were placed in the background, as subordinates or accessories to a white person, or they were a part of a larger composition. But not here. Her pose and grace resemble the traditional portraits that were regularly of white women.

meta.video_title: 10 Artworks to See at the Louvre Museum | Behind the Masterpiece

text: Madeleine is theoretically free, but this decree was fairly recent and thus only partially applied so this portrait of a black woman was the first of its kind. She sits against an empty background, draped in a white  garment that slips from her shoulders to reveal her right breast. Her hair covered, in an elaborate white head wrap. Her eyes gaze directly at us. Perhaps feelings of unrest come up, of looking and being looked at. In choosing to paint a woman of color, Benoist engaged in a politically contentious issue, one that her own husband  had a vested interest in, as upper-class white couple. This representation was historically significant. But it’s important to keep in mind that at the time, the artist being as a white woman, had much more agency here than subject, despite Madeleine’s assured demeanour. The Louvre has an incredibly rich collection of artworks, but that hasn’t stopped them in acquiring modern and contemporary pieces. One of my favourite, and underrated new additions is this piece by Anselm Kiefer. This monumental painting depicts a naked man lying flat on

meta.video_title: 10 Artworks to See at the Louvre Museum | Behind the Masterpiece

text: his back, staring up at a star-filled sky. A faint beam of light runs from his chest up to the constellations above him. Its name comes from a type of furnace used  by alchemists that transformed base metal to gold. This chemical process is beautifully depicted in this composition.  Through the material Kiefer chose to use. The three colors on this canvas, black, white and red correspond to the three colors of this chemical process, lead, silver and gold. We can see and almost feel the rough texture here. He uses specs of silver at the top that transitions into gold as we pan down, much like the chemical process behind its name. Our gaze finally rests at the bottom, where the figure lies on a cracked layer of red soil. The artist actually poured lead over this section, keeping true to the work’s inspiration. When asked about the meaning of this pieces, Kiefer responded saying it is his self-portrait. But the figure is not dead, rather he is “in the universe.” His contribution to the museum, was followed by addition of other contemporary artists to the Louvre’s permanent collection, including Cy Twombly, and François Morellet. Visiting

The Seine is a river that flows through the heart of Paris with many of  the city's most famous monuments built right along its scenic banks. On a single walk here, you'll  get to see architectural knockouts like the Notre Dame, the Louvre, Pont Alexandre and even the Eiffel Tower,  among many other impressive monuments. Another must-do on this side of the river is, of course, a wander  through the Latin Quarter or the Quartier Latin. This historic student district is one of the oldest  in the city, best known for its narrow cobblestone streets, lively cafés and vibrant atmosphere, with a  name, of course, that references the prominence of Latin as a spoken language here back in the Middle  Ages. There are a lot of highlights to enjoy in the Latin Quarter, the most famous of which is no  doubt the Panthéon; a neoclassical mausoleum that is absolutely breathtaking on the inside.  Completed in 1790 and originally intended to be a church, today the Panthéon is best known for  the many iconic French figures who are interred here, like Marie Curie, Voltaire, Rousseau and Victor  Hugo. Other highlights include the beautiful Saint- Michel Fountain, as well as the Sorbonne building and,  of course, there's quite a few nice museums here as well, including the Musée de Cluny, one of the world's  most extensive collections of medieval art and artifacts; as well as the Museum of Natural History,  housed in the beautiful Jardin des plantes. While in

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN PARIS FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20+ Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: this area, history nerds can also check out the  Arènes de Lutèce, an ancient amphitheater dating back to the 1st Century. Or for something a bit more  offbeat to do in Paris, you can head to the Grande Mosquée de Paris, a stunning architectural masterpiece inspired  by Moorish design, filled with intricate tile work, lush gardens and serene courtyards. In terms  of iconic parks, this part of Paris has another beautiful option for picnics and general strolling:  the incredible Luxembourg Gardens, a verdant oasis in the heart of Paris with tree-and-flower lined  paths, a big pond where visitors play with toy sailboats and the elegant Luxembourg Palace which  today is home to the French Senate. Another historic neighborhood to explore just north of here  is the gorgeous quartier Saint-Germain-des-Prés, renowned for its literary cafes, galleries and plenty of shopping  opportunities. Granted, these days Cafe de Flore and Les Deux Magots are more tourist hubs than rogue  meeting places for haunted creatives but they are pretty, just like the rest of the neighborhood,  with key highlights like Église Saint-Sulpice ready for your nosy perusal. Honestly, the best way to enjoy this  neighborhood, like with many others, is to just soak

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN PARIS FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20+ Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: up the atmosphere while dreamily walking around.  Now, if you spent a comically disproportionate amount of time learning about Napoleon in history  class, another interesting attraction in Paris is his tomb which is fittingly, disproportionately  large. You can find this grandiose sarcophagus in the iconic gold-topped Dôme des Invalides, which was actually  the tallest structure in Paris before the Eiffel Tower turned up. On that note, of course, a highlight  of the seventh arrondissement is the most famous symbol of Paris: the Eiffel Tower. At the time of its  construction for the 1889 World's Fair in Paris, it was actually the tallest man-made structure  in the world. A title it held until 1930 when it was dethroned by the Chrysler Building. While  considered by many to be an eyesore initially, there's no doubt that the Eiffel Tower today is  synonymous with Paris, so you definitely have to see it. If you're short on time, I'd say there's no  need to pay and wait in line to go up the tower. After all, the most iconic views of Paris contain  the Eiffel Tower in them, which makes the views up there feel weirdly incomplete. Instead, remember  you can get great Eiffel Tower views from tons of different areas, the most popular being from  Trocadero or Champ de Mars, but with plenty of side

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN PARIS FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20+ Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: streets and bridges offering unique vantage points as  well, with a fraction of the crowds, so don't be afraid to explore and branch out. A classic view  can be found on the line six Metro between the Passy and Bir Hakeim stations. Now, it's time to move  on to things to do in arrondissements 8 and 9. We'll start with the classic Arc de Triomphe, a monumental triumphal Arch that commemorates France's military victories. This is probably one of the most famous sites in the  entire city, at least as far as photo ops are concerned. And while almost everyone comes here  for a photo, far fewer actually make the visit up to the top, which I think is a shame because the  views are truly stunning. Just be warned that there is a tunnel that takes you over to the arch and  you're not meant to sprint across the roundabout, as you'll see many tourists doing. From here, you  can enjoy a walk along one of Paris's most famous boulevards: the Champs-Élysées, forever immortalized  in a classic song of the same name. To be honest, this isn't the best street to shop or eat  in Paris with many tourist traps along the way but it is still an iconic stop and worth doing if  you continue your walk till the end, which is when you'll reach one of the largest public squares in  Paris: Place de la Concord. With a golden Egyptian obelisk

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN PARIS FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20+ Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: as its centerpiece and two dreamy decorative  fountains, this is a beautiful square well worth seeing. Not so beautiful, however, is its history;  this square was famously the execution site of many notable figures during the French Revolution,  including Marie Antoinette, though today it's mainly just a nice spot for photos. From here, you're  about a 15-minute walk away from one of the most beautiful attractions in Paris: the Palais  Garnier, also known as the Paris Opera House and yes, the real-life lair of the Phantom of  the Opera! This legendary opera house is a masterpiece of 19th-century architecture and while  the grandiose facade is nice to look at, you really do need to step inside for the full impact  with its magnificent grand staircase, dazzling chandeliers and sumptuous gilded decor all around.  Even if you can't experience a live show in this cultural landmark, I can highly recommend booking  a ticket to see the inside because it's honestly stunning. Close by you'll also find some of  Paris's most luxurious department stores: Galeries Lafayette and Printemps. Even if you're not  a fan of designer shopping, a visit to these two is worth it if, even just to admire their ornate  Belle Époque interiors and glorious rooftop views.

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN PARIS FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20+ Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: Finally, let's move on to more things to do in  Paris outside of the central arrondissements. There are, of course, so many but I'll just highlight  some of my favorites. First, I'd say every visitor needs to visit Montmartre, once a village filled with  artists, today a neighborhood filled with tourists. That said, touristy as it may be these days, it's  still one of the most beautiful neighborhoods in Paris. Highlights include the iconic Sacré-Cœur Basilica  with its striking white domes and panoramic views, artist-filled Place du Tertre, the adorable wall of  I Love Yous and, of course, the famous Moulin Rouge. Honestly, though, the best thing to do here is  to just explore and wander around. While it does get intensely busy, there are still some cool sites  and side streets to take in where you can actually escape the crowds, especially if you come early, I'm  talking sunrise early but it honestly is so worth it. Next, just north of the 18th you'll find  the delightful Marché aux Puces de Paris Saint-Ouen, known as one of the largest and oldest flea markets in the world,  here visitors can browse through eclectic stalls selling everything from retro clothing and  antique furniture to quirky collectibles and

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN PARIS FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20+ Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: unique souvenirs. Definitely a really cool spot  to check out. Out in the 10th there's another great area worth exploring: Canal Saint-Martin. This  picturesque waterway is lined with fun cafes, shops and restaurants making it a go-to spot for  locals and tourists alike to enjoy an afternoon or evening. If you're looking for a lively place  to hang out in Paris with a chiller vibe than the tourist center, this is a great pick. Close to  here you'll also find one of Paris's most famous squares: Place de la Bastille, built on the site of the  former prison of the same name, whose historic storming played a key role in the early days of  the French Revolution. Today there's not much to do here apart from admire the Colonne de Juillet monument but it may still be of interest for history buffs. Over in the 11th you'll also find the Atelier  des Lumières: a super cool exhibition space known for their immersive multimedia installations, housed  in a former steel foundry, of course, for optimal cool points. Today, it's home to floor to ceiling  projections that bring iconic artworks to life with music and dynamic animations. I went  to one about Van Gogh and it was honestly amazing. Now out in the 14th you'll find the  world's most famous ossuary: the Catacombs of

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN PARIS FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20+ Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: Paris, a spooky attraction perfect for those who  are looking for low-key creepy things to do. This macabre underground labyrinth is said to contain  the bones of about six million Parisians, a spooky solution for the increasingly overcrowded  cemeteries of the late 18th century. Today, a small portion of these tunnels are open to the public,  allowing you to frolic among a sea of skulls and bones, if that's your kind of thing. Now, sticking  out like a dark sore thumb in Paris's 15th arrondissement is the Tour Montparnasse: a tower often referred to as the ugliest building in Paris. This tower is actually Paris's tallest skyscraper and therefore offers sweeping  views of the city's iconic landmarks and skyline though, to be honest, there's tons of great views  around the city, so I wouldn't necessarily go out of my way to come here. In terms of beautiful parks  though, one of my favorites in Paris can be found in the 19th: the Parc des Buttes-Chaumont, a whimsical parkland featuring dramatic cliffs, cascading waterfalls and picturesque bridges. Definitely a great place  to escape the tourist masses for an afternoon. Finally, out in the 20th is the historic  Pére Lachaise Cemetery, the final resting place of

meta.video_title: BEST THINGS TO DO IN PARIS FOR FIRST TIMERS W/ MAP | 20+ Must-Dos, Hidden Gems & More!

text: countless notable figures from the worlds of  art, literature and politics, including Oscar Wild, Jim Morrison and Édith Piaf. Sure, visiting  a cemetery seems like a strange thing to do but if you have the time this is definitely a  peaceful and beautiful corner of the city to put on your list. Now, speaking of lists, for  a full list with way more recommendations, as well as a map with all these activities,  be sure to check out the written version of this guide. Thank you so much for  watching, I'll see you guys next week, bye!

text: Paris, a city of legend and romance. And at its heart, a museum with secrets dark and strange. Tales of murder and conspiracy. A lady who vanishes. The cunning lies of an emperor. And the greatest survival story ever told. [cheering] Secrets hidden in plain sight inside the L. Paris is an ancient city, witness to revolution, celebration, and war. At its center stands the Louvre, once a royal palace, and now one of the world's greatest museums, the Louvre is home to the most famous painting in human history.

meta.video_title: The Dark Side of the Louvre

text: The Mona Lisa. Her enigmatic smile is said to conceal many secrets. One of them is how she became so famous. For years, she was just one among many masterpieces at the Louva. But then, on a summer day in 1911, the lady vanished. This was the greatest art theft ever committed. You know, it was unprecedented. It made worldwide headlines. Now, the Mona Lisa had been a famous painting, but it's not the Mona Lisa that we know today. It's not the icon that you see on coffee mugs and t-shirts and then being sold at souvenir stands. The Mona Lisa was not that well known. Today, 8 million visitors a year are drawn here by her fame and mystery. [music] But in 1911, she caught the eye of one man who had his own more secret motives. Vincenzo Purua, he was one of the guys that was brought to the Lou to put the masterpieces under glass.

meta.video_title: The Dark Side of the Louvre

text: As he was walking around, [music] he would notice all this great Italian artwork and he kind of wondered where did it come from. He heard some stories, he read some things and he put two and two together and and kind of got five out of it. He got into his head that he was going to return a masterpiece to Italy where he thought it belonged. And the one that he chose was the Mona Lisa. If you've seen any [music] of these great heist movies, it's carefully planned by a small gang. They plot everything out. They've got their escape route planned. Perujia, as far as I can tell, didn't do any of that. Peruja simply waited for a Monday when the Louv was closed to the public, then entered with all the other workers. He went to the salon kare. The guard was outside taking a cigarette break. Aruja took the Mona Lisa off the wall and carried it to a service staircase where he took apart the frame he'd helped to build. He tried to get out a locked door by taking the doorork knob off, but it didn't work. So, he turned around, walked back through the louva, and left

meta.video_title: The Dark Side of the Louvre

text: through the same door he'd come in.
